one_line_takeaway: Vendor claims of 'hallucination-free' legal AI are overstated: preregistered testing shows leading RAG-based legal research tools still hallucinate 17-33% of the time, with subtle mis-cited-authority errors that are especially hard for lawyers to catch.
key_finding: RAG reduces but does not eliminate hallucination in legal AI. Overall response breakdown: Lexis+ AI 65% accurate / 18% incomplete / 17% hallucinated; Westlaw AI-AR 42% accurate / 25% incomplete / 33% hallucinated; Ask Practical Law AI 20% accurate / 63% incomplete / 17% hallucinated; GPT-4 49% accurate / 8% incomplete / 43% hallucinated. So the specialized tools hallucinate far less than GPT-4 (which the paper elsewhere notes prior work put at 58-82% on legal queries), but Westlaw hallucinates about one-third of the time -- roughly twice the other legal tools -- and Ask Practical Law AI's low hallucination rate comes at the cost of refusing/ungrounded answers on over 60% of queries. Beyond raw rates, the paper's qualitative analysis shows hallucinations are 'insidious': systems misstate holdings (sometimes the exact opposite of what a case held), confuse the arguments of litigants with the court's holding, violate the hierarchy of legal authority (e.g., Westlaw claiming a state supreme court 'reversed' a U.S. Supreme Court decision on federal law), and fabricate nonexistent statutory provisions. Misgrounded citations (a real case cited but not supporting the claim) are argued to be MORE dangerous than outright fabricated cases because they are harder to detect. Error-cause prevalence among hallucinations (non-exclusive, Table 6): Lexis -- naive retrieval 0.47, inapplicable authority 0.38, reasoning error 0.28, sycophancy 0.06; Westlaw -- 0.20 / 0.23 / 0.61 / 0.00; Practical Law -- 0.34 / 0.34 / 0.49 / 0.03. Westlaw's high rate is partly attributed to it generating the longest answers (mean 350 words vs 219 for Lexis, 175 for Practical Law), giving more falsifiable propositions.
core_contribution: The first preregistered, systematic empirical evaluation of leading proprietary, RAG-based AI legal research tools (LexisNexis's Lexis+ AI, Thomson Reuters's Ask Practical Law AI, and Westlaw's AI-Assisted Research), benchmarked against closed-book GPT-4. The paper's own four contributions are: (1) the first performance assessment of RAG-based commercial legal AI tools on real-world research tasks; (2) a hand-built, preregistered dataset of ~200 (202) open-ended legal queries designed to probe RAG-resistant failures; (3) a refined typology that decomposes 'factual hallucination' into two dimensions -- correctness and groundedness -- yielding operational labels (correct/incorrect/refusal; grounded/misgrounded/ungrounded; a response is 'hallucinated' if incorrect OR misgrounded, 'accurate' if correct AND grounded, 'incomplete' if a refusal or correct-but-ungrounded); and (4) a typology of RAG error causes (naive retrieval, inapplicable authority, reasoning error, sycophancy) plus implications for lawyers' professional duties. The headline empirical finding is that vendor 'hallucination-free'/'eliminated hallucinations' marketing claims are overstated: the legal tools hallucinate 17%-33% of the time.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is an empirical reliability/trustworthiness evaluation, not an adversarial security study. The paper defines no attacker, no adversarial capability, and no threat model. 'Hallucination' here means factual error or false citation-support under ordinary (non-adversarial) legal research use; queries are honest research questions (including some with an innocently mistaken premise), not crafted attacks. The relevance to AI-security is indirect: it concerns the trustworthiness and failure modes of deployed high-stakes AI whose vendors make unverified safety claims.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) only three products evaluated; the fast-growing startup space (Harvey, Vincent AI) is even more access-restricted. (2) Point-in-time snapshot -- responses (especially Lexis+ AI) evolved during the study; results do not speak to LexisNexis's later 'second generation' tool. (3) The framework covers chat-style research; it does not evaluate other generative legal tasks (memo drafting, deposition summaries, contract review). (4) Small sample (202 queries) relative to prior work, driven by restricted vendor access and expensive manual evaluation. (5) Groundedness is really a spectrum (an overruled case may still be a useful research starting point) though coded categorically as misgrounded. (6) The benchmark is deliberately challenging and not meant as an unbiased estimate of the population hallucination rate -- the goal is to test whether RAG has 'solved' hallucination, not to estimate real-world query distributions. Reviewer-noted caveats: vendor systems are non-deterministic and their retrieval parameters/prompts are opaque, so exact failure attribution is impossible; test-leakage/memorization is a concern since the only access is sending prompts to the providers; and the paper is a June 2024 preprint 'under review.' The text also contains minor drafting artifacts (e.g., a garbled sentence 'Last, We not only uncovers...').

datasets_used: Authors' own preregistered benchmark of 202 hand-built legal queries in 4 categories: general legal research (n=80), jurisdiction/time-specific (n=70), false-premise (n=22), factual recall (n=30)
20 queries reused verbatim from LegalBench's Rule QA task (Guha et al., 2023)
20 BARBRI bar-exam prep questions reused verbatim (BARBRI, Inc., 2013)
Preregistration filed with the Open Science Framework (Surani et al., 2024)
quantitative_results: Overall accurate rate: Lexis+ AI 65%, Westlaw AI-AR 42%, Ask Practical Law AI 20%, GPT-4 49%
Overall hallucination rate: Lexis+ AI 17%, Westlaw AI-AR 33%, Ask Practical Law AI 17%, GPT-4 43%
Overall incomplete rate: Lexis+ AI 18%, Westlaw AI-AR 25%, Ask Practical Law AI 63% (text also cites 62%), GPT-4 8%
Legal tools hallucinate 'between 17% and 33% of the time' (abstract headline)
Inter-rater reliability: Cohen's kappa = 0.77, 85.4% agreement on final label over 48 re-coded responses
Average answer length excluding refusals: Westlaw 350 words (SD=120), Lexis+ AI 219 (SD=114), Ask Practical Law AI 175 (SD=67)
Hallucination-cause prevalence (Lexis / Westlaw / Practical Law): naive retrieval 0.47/0.20/0.34; inapplicable authority 0.38/0.23/0.34; reasoning error 0.28/0.61/0.49; sycophancy 0.06/0.00/0.03
Prior work (Dahl et al., 2024) cited: general-purpose LLMs hallucinate on legal queries 58-82% of the time
Ask Practical Law AI accuracy rose from 19% to 20% after a second validation round (within inter-rater bounds)
baselines_compared: GPT-4 (gpt-4-turbo-2024-04-09), closed-book via OpenAI API, as the general-purpose reference point
LexisNexis Lexis+ AI (RAG over full LexisNexis case law/codes/rules/treatises)
Thomson Reuters Ask Practical Law AI (RAG restricted to curated Practical Law editorial documents)
Westlaw AI-Assisted Research / AI-AR (RAG over Westlaw cases/statutes/regulations/headnotes/KeyCite, appears built on GPT-4)
